p.seg: To all sons of the Church who shall view or hear the present writing, Walter son of Alan, steward of the king of Scotland, sends greeting.

I, in honour of God and for the peace of the Church, and mainly wishing to provide tranquility at Melrose, have thought fit to clarify doubts once contained in the charter of Walter my grandfather given to the monks of Melrose, in that part of the charter, that is, where my said grandfather spoke of untilled lands and haughs and of forest pasture. This I also will, that where there is contained less of charity and security, in this instrument to describe more clearly, and to provide for the monks more fully.

I have therefore granted to the aforementioned monks all untilled lands and haughs, as well in my forests as outside, on the northern side of the River Ayr, and my whole forest to be used according to what should seem the more useful to them, that is, to be tilled and sown wherever it should seem to them to be suitable, as well within the forest as outside, and for the feeding and pasturing and constantly keeping their flocks within the same forest, as many as they wish, according to what the territory of the same forest is able to contain; and it shall be in the judgment of the monks to calculate how much the same land is able to support. I have also granted to them the use of the same forest in all its easements and benefits; to be had and possessed fully and peaceably for all their own needs; this alone excepted, that [neither] the monks nor the lay-brothers nor any other of their men under authority shall hunt or catch sparrow-hawks in the same forest; for this is not appropriate to their order, not do we believe it to be beneficial for them. All these things have I given, granted, and made firm by this my charter, to God and the aforementioned monks. And I, and my heirs and successors all, shall warrant and defend for the aforesaid monks all the above-said things and we shall cause them to possess them in peace, free and quit from every service and exaction and secular custom.
And so that my grant should be firm and unimpaired, I have handed over the present writing confirmed with my own present seal.

With these witnesses: William, my chaplain; Malcolm, son of the earl of Lennox, my nephew; John of Montgomery; Roger son of Glay; William of Halkerston; Alan Wallace; Humphrey del Bois; John of Lindsay; Hugh, son of Simon son of Bertin; and many others.
